extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('p') (line 39) is directly concatenated into a file path via os.path.join(base_path, filename) and opened with open(). No sanitization or validation prevents path traversal sequences (../), allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files on the server filesystem such as /etc/passwd or the Django settings.py containing the SECRET_KEY.

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

extra.message: User input from request.POST.get('first_name') (line 68) is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec(), allowing arbitrary Python code execution on the server. An attacker can execute any Python code, e.g., writing files, accessing the database, or spawning a reverse shell.

User input from request.POST.get('first_name') is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec() in the Python 2 fallback path. Same arbitrary code execution vulnerability as line 72 but for the Python 2 compatibility branch.

User input from request.GET.get('qs') is passed in the template context and rendered with the |safe filter in form.html line 28 (value="{{qs|safe}}"), disabling Django's auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript through the qs query parameter, enabling reflected XSS.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from the URL path parameter is passed in the template context and rendered with the |safe filter inside a JavaScript string in path.html line 49 (var myvar = "{{path|safe}}";). This allows an attacker to break out of the JavaScript string and inject arbitrary script code via the URL path.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('qs') is passed in the template context and rendered with the |safe filter inside a JavaScript string in query.html line 37 (var myvar = "{{qs|safe}}";). This allows an attacker to break out of the JavaScript string and inject arbitrary script code via the qs query parameter.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('qs') is passed in the template context and rendered with the |safe filter inside a JavaScript string in image.html line 48 (var x = "{{qs|safe}}";). This allows an attacker to break out of the JavaScript string and inject arbitrary script code via the qs query parameter.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The dor_user_profile view accesses and modifies user data based on the userid parameter from the URL without any authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated user can view and modify any other user's profile (including the admin user at id='2') by changing the userid in the URL. POST requests at lines 133-134 directly modify user data with no ownership verification.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The missing_access_control view grants admin access based solely on the GET parameter action=admin with no authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated user can access the admin interface by adding ?action=admin to the URL.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The @csrf_exempt decorator on csrf_image disables Django's CSRF protection for this view. Combined with the XSS vulnerability in the same view (qs parameter rendered with |safe), this allows an attacker to forge requests to this endpoint from a victim's browser.

User input from request.GET.get('url') is passed directly to Django's redirect() without any validation or allowlist checking. This allows an attacker to redirect users to any external URL, enabling phishing attacks.

cwe: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('fwd') is used to look up and call arbitrary functions via globals().get(forward). An attacker can invoke any function in the module's global scope, including admin() (rendering the admin page), code_execution() (executing arbitrary Python), or other internal functions not exposed via URL routes.

Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in the source code as 'h++jszpm)i@p%ay_b=cp#()^od!qns14)h%@qm3)p=cuo+st^a'. This key is used for cryptographic signing of sessions, CSRF tokens, and password reset tokens. Exposure allows session hijacking, CSRF token forgery, and other attacks.

DEBUG = True is set, which causes Django to display detailed error pages with stack traces, local variables, settings values (including SECRET_KEY), and SQL queries on any unhandled exception. This exposes sensitive application internals to attackers.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: The CsrfViewMiddleware is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES, meaning CSRF protection is globally disabled for the entire application. All POST requests are vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks without per-view exemptions being needed.

The show_toolbar function enables the Django Debug Toolbar for any request that includes 'debug' in GET parameters, POST data, or cookies. This allows any unauthenticated user to activate the toolbar and view sensitive application internals including SQL queries, settings, and request/response data.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: The boom view raises a generic Exception. Combined with DEBUG=True (settings.py:10), this causes Django to render a detailed error page exposing stack traces, local variables, settings (including SECRET_KEY), and SQL queries to the attacker.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
